title: Writing a Research Proposal - Fill in the Blank

questions.question: Name the document type whose only job is to get a not-yet-run study approved.
questions.blank_sentence: A ___ is a plan for a study you haven't run yet, written to get it green-lit.
questions.explanation: A proposal is a plan for a future study whose only job is to get it approved. Unlike a paper, it contains no results and no discussion.

questions.question: Name the two sections a paper has that a proposal does not (give the one that reports outcomes).
questions.blank_sentence: A proposal has no discussion and no ___, because the study has not yet been run.
questions.explanation: A proposal lacks both results and discussion since it plans a future study. Results and Discussion appear only in the paper, which reports an already-conducted study.

questions.question: Name the informal test that checks whether the motivation made the reader care.
questions.blank_sentence: If a reader can shrug at your question, you have failed the '___?' test.
questions.explanation: The 'so what?' test checks the motivation: if the reader can shrug, the motivation has failed to establish relevance. It is the diagnostic for the single biggest proposal error, missing motivation.

questions.question: Name the quality the methods section must convince the reader of (the key replication test).
questions.blank_sentence: Methods must show the study is correct and ___, so someone could repeat it from the description.
questions.explanation: The methods section must convince the reader the approach is correct and replicable. The key test is whether someone could replicate your study from the description alone.

questions.question: Name the way of using sources that explains why they were all invited together, telling a story.
questions.blank_sentence: Explaining what holds your sources together and what each uniquely adds is ___, not summary.
questions.explanation: Synthesis explains why sources were invited together and what each uniquely adds, telling a story. A one-by-one introduction of each source is merely a summary.

questions.question: Name the most common, hardest-to-master source-use error (the 'Smith said X, Jones said Y' pattern).
questions.blank_sentence: Writing 'Smith said X. Furthermore, Jones said Y.' is ___ instead of synthesising.
questions.explanation: Listing sources one after another with no narrative is the most common and hardest source-use error. The fix is to synthesise: connect the sources around your own point.

questions.question: Name the 5 C that means stating which arguments were more convincing and what is lacking.
questions.blank_sentence: Judging which arguments held up and what is missing is the C called ___.
questions.explanation: Critique means assessing which arguments were more convincing and why, and what is lacking. It is one of the 5 C's alongside Cite, Compare, Contrast, and Connect.

questions.question: Name the chart that sorts ideas across sources and is read horizontally for synthesis.
questions.blank_sentence: A ___ matrix puts sources across the top and ideas down the side; read it horizontally.
questions.explanation: A synthesis matrix sorts similarities and differences across the literature on one topic. Reading horizontally (one idea across sources) yields synthesis, while reading vertically yields a summary.

questions.question: Name the give-away signal that reveals AI was used to find sources.
questions.blank_sentence: The give-away of AI source-finding is the ___ source, which does not really exist.
questions.explanation: Hallucinated sources are the main AI risk: the tool invents both the source and its contents. This is why finding sources with AI is forbidden and every citation must trace to a real paper you read.

questions.question: Name the separate declaration of any GenAI or tooling that must be attached to the proposal.
questions.blank_sentence: Besides references, the proposal must attach the ___ Statement.
questions.explanation: The Technology Statement is a required attachment declaring any GenAI or tooling used. It is one of the explicit assignment requirements alongside references and a max-3-page limit.
